summary: Heavily obfuscated bulk-email/SMS spam toolkit shipped under hidden `.ad/` directory
details: The package advertises itself as red-team email/SMS tooling (package.json keywords include `red-team` and `smtp`) and ships ~46 runtime modules under a dot-prefixed hidden directory `.ad/`, every file wrapped with obfuscator.io string-array + base64/RC4 string decoders. The `main` entry `.ad/x0.js` is a commander CLI that invokes `program.parse()` at module top level, so a bare `require('nolimit-x')` will dispatch CLI handling on the host process argv. Functionality includes bulk SMS-via-SMTP carrier gateways (`sendViaSmtpGateway`, Plivo/MessageBird in `.ad/sms-providers.js`), Puppeteer injection of a custom sending panel into open Chrome webmail sessions (`.ad/web-command.js`, `web` subcommand), and contact-harvesting from authenticated Microsoft Graph (`/v1.0/me/contacts?$top=1000`) and Google People accounts plus GitHub/Gravatar enrichment of recipient addresses. A user-invoked `activate` subcommand POSTs `{key, hwid, hostname, platform}` to an RC4-encoded `LICENSE_SERVER` URL — this is a standard commercial license-gating shape, narrow in data and triggered by the user. No remote-code fetch, no installer-secret reads (`~/.aws`, `~/.ssh`, `~/.npmrc`, browser profiles), no install-time lifecycle hooks, and no exfiltration of caller-supplied data to attacker infrastructure were observed. The harm surface is to third parties (recipients of unsolicited mail/SMS, harvested contacts of authenticated sender accounts), not to the developer who installs the package. Routed for human review because uniformly obfuscated source for an openly-advertised spam/abuse toolkit is a registry-policy decision rather than a clear-cut installer-side supply-chain attack.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
